_comment: Point-in-time events in Earth's history. 'ma' = millions of years ago (a single moment; for long episodes like ice ages use a representative date). 'text' is a SHORT description shown in the hover PREVIEW card. Optional 'overview' (a longer intro) and 'info' (free-form key/value facts, like a period's info) fill the bigger DETAIL modal that opens when you click the marker — if 'overview' is absent the modal falls back to 'text', and 'info' is skipped if missing. 'image' is an optional 1:1 picture at assets/images/events/<id>.jpg (used in the hover/detail cards; a placeholder shows if missing). Optional 'background' is a larger/landscape image used as the page background when the event is shown (clicked, or reached in the journey); it falls back to 'image' if absent. 'category' is an optional label (e.g. impact, extinction) shown as a small tag. To add an event: copy an entry and fill it in.

events.name: End of the Late Heavy Bombardment
events.text: The intense asteroid and comet bombardment that scarred the young Earth and Moon tapers off, leaving the surface calmer and allowing stable crust and early oceans to persist.

events.name: The Great Oxidation Event
events.text: Photosynthesising cyanobacteria flood the atmosphere with free oxygen for the first time, rusting the oceans into banded iron formations and driving many anaerobic microbes to extinction.

events.name: Snowball Earth (Marinoan glaciation)
events.text: Global glaciation drives ice sheets nearly to the equator, locking much of the planet in ice. Its end is followed soon after by the rise of the first complex multicellular life.

events.name: The Great Dying (end-Permian extinction)
events.text: The most severe mass extinction in Earth's history. Massive Siberian volcanism wipes out roughly 90% of marine species and 70% of land vertebrates.

events.name: Chicxulub impact (K–Pg extinction)
events.text: A roughly 10 km asteroid strikes the Yucatán, triggering a global winter that ends the non-avian dinosaurs and opens the world to the rise of mammals.
events.overview: On an otherwise ordinary day at the close of the Cretaceous, a mountain-sized asteroid slammed into the shallow sea off the Yucatán Peninsula at tens of kilometres per second. The impact flung molten rock around the globe; the falling debris heated the sky and ignited wildfires, then plunged the world into a years-long 'impact winter' as soot and aerosols blocked the Sun. Photosynthesis collapsed on land and in the oceans, and roughly three-quarters of all species — every non-avian dinosaur among them — vanished, clearing the way for mammals to inherit the Earth.
